does: This paper proposes establishing an international consortium dedicated to evaluating societal-scale risks from advanced AI systems. The consortium would combine resources and expertise from both AI developers and independent third-party evaluators to systematically assess frontier AI capabilities and potential harms. The core contribution is identifying and articulating why a coordinated, multi-stakeholder approach is necessary to address the current regulatory gap where AI labs operate with minimal oversight. The paper diagnoses specific failures in the existing evaluation ecosystem—limited evaluator diversity, inefficient effort allocation, and misaligned incentives—and proposes a consortium structure as a concrete governance mechanism to overcome these coordination problems and enable rigorous assessment of advanced AI risks at scale.
connects: This work builds on emerging literature on AI governance, regulatory frameworks for emerging technologies, and recent proposals for frontier AI system evaluations developed in response to capabilities advances in large language models and other systems. It extends prior work on AI risk assessment methodologies and safety evaluation protocols by shifting focus toward the governance and institutional structures needed to implement evaluations effectively. The paper responds to earlier research identifying regulatory gaps and coordination failures in the AI development ecosystem, synthesizing insights from technology policy, international governance arrangements, and multi-stakeholder coordination frameworks to apply them specifically to frontier AI safety evaluation.
enables: This proposal establishes foundations for designing practical international governance mechanisms for advanced AI development. It opens pathways for developing standardized evaluation protocols and shared infrastructure that multiple evaluators could employ, reducing duplication and improving consistency. The consortium approach enables future work on harmonizing evaluation standards across jurisdictions, creating accountability mechanisms for AI developers, and establishing institutional capacity for continuous assessment of emerging capabilities and risks. It points toward broader questions about appropriate institutional structures for governing transformative technologies and could inform models for international coordination on other existential risks, while creating opportunities to empirically test what governance structures effectively influence frontier AI development practices.
